description: Eval tests for /rune:appraise multi-agent code review skill

evals.prompt: Run /rune:appraise on my current branch changes. Walk me through what happens in Phase 0 (Pre-flight) and Phase 1 (Rune Gaze). Explain: (1) How are changed files detected? (2) What is the file classification algorithm? (3) How are Ashes selected for each file type? (4) What is the dry-run exit path? Don't actually execute anything — just explain the decision tree.
evals.expected_output: A detailed walkthrough of: (1) Phase 0 Pre-flight — detect default_branch from git remote, build changed_files list (committed + staged + unstaged + untracked or staged-only for --partial), filter non-existent files and symlinks, generate diff ranges for Phase 5.3 scope tagging. Abort conditions: no changed files or only non-reviewable files. (2) Phase 1 Rune Gaze — classify files by extension (Python → Forge Warden backend review; TypeScript → Glyph Scribe frontend review; all files → Ward Sentinel security, Pattern Weaver quality, Veil Piercer truthbinding), discover custom Ashes from talisman.yml, add UX reviewers when talisman.ux.enabled + frontend files detected. Always-on Ashes: Ward Sentinel, Pattern Weaver, Veil Piercer. Dry-run exits after Phase 1 with summary.
expectations: Describes Phase 0 changed file detection from git diff
Mentions default_branch detection from git remote
Lists the 4 sources of changed files: committed, staged, unstaged, untracked
Notes --partial flag limits to staged-only files
Describes Phase 1 file classification by extension
Mentions always-on Ashes: Ward Sentinel, Pattern Weaver, Veil Piercer
Mentions Forge Warden for backend files
Mentions Glyph Scribe for frontend files
Mentions custom Ash discovery from talisman.yml
Describes UX reviewer conditional addition based on talisman.ux.enabled
Notes dry-run exits after Phase 1 with summary

evals.prompt: Analyze the --deep flag behavior for /rune:appraise. Explain the 3 waves of review. What Ashes are in each wave? What is the wave scheduling algorithm? How does it interact with other flags like --max-agents and --no-converge?
evals.expected_output: Deep review analysis: Wave 1 (Core, up to 7 Ashes) — standard review Ashes selected by Rune Gaze (forge-warden, ward-sentinel, pattern-weaver, veil-piercer, plus conditional glyph-scribe, knowledge-keeper, codex-oracle). Wave 2 (Investigation, 4 Ashes) — rot-seeker, strand-tracer, decree-auditor, fringe-watcher. Wave 3 (Dimension, 7 Ashes) — truth-seeker, ruin-watcher, breach-hunter, order-auditor, ember-seer, signal-watcher, decay-tracer. Wave scheduling: each wave runs as full Roundtable Circle pass with its own team, tasks, monitor cycle. --max-agents applies to Wave 1 only — Wave 2/3 are deepOnly and not subject to cap. --no-converge affects per-chunk convergence, not wave scheduling. Deep mode runs up to 18 Ashes total across 3 waves.
expectations: Describes Wave 1 as Core with up to 7 standard review Ashes
Lists Wave 2 Investigation Ashes: rot-seeker, strand-tracer, decree-auditor, fringe-watcher
Lists Wave 3 Dimension Ashes: truth-seeker, ruin-watcher, breach-hunter, order-auditor, ember-seer, signal-watcher, decay-tracer
Notes each wave runs as full Roundtable Circle pass
Explains --max-agents applies to Wave 1 only
Notes Wave 2/3 are deepOnly and not subject to cap
Explains --no-converge affects per-chunk convergence not wave scheduling
Mentions total up to 18 Ashes across 3 waves

evals.prompt: Explain Phase 2 (Forge Team) for /rune:appraise. What is the session-scoped identifier format? What fields go into the state file for session isolation? What is inscription.json and what does it contain?
evals.expected_output: Phase 2 Forge Team breakdown: Session identifier format: {gitHash}-{shortSession} where gitHash is first 7 chars of HEAD commit and shortSession is first 8 chars of CLAUDE_SESSION_ID. State file fields (session isolation): config_dir (CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR path), owner_pid ($PPID for session isolation), session_id (CLAUDE_SESSION_ID), workflow ('rune-review'), scope ('diff'), depth ('standard' or 'deep'), identifier, created_at, status. inscription.json contains: diff_scope (file paths with line ranges for Phase 5.3 tagging), context_intelligence (PR metadata, linked issues from Phase 0.3), linter_context (discovered linters from Phase 0.4), risk_map (from Phase 0.5 Lore Layer if enabled).
expectations: Describes identifier format: {gitHash}-{shortSession}
Notes gitHash is first 7 chars of HEAD commit
Notes shortSession is first 8 chars of CLAUDE_SESSION_ID
Lists state file fields: config_dir, owner_pid, session_id, workflow, scope, depth, identifier, created_at, status
Explains config_dir is for installation isolation
Explains owner_pid is for session isolation via $PPID
Describes inscription.json contents: diff_scope, context_intelligence, linter_context, risk_map
Notes diff_scope is used for Phase 5.3 scope tagging

evals.prompt: How does Phase 3 (Summon Ash) work in /rune:appraise? What is the prompt generation contract? How are built-in vs custom Ashes handled? What security measures protect against prompt injection via file paths?
evals.expected_output: Phase 3 Summon Ash: Prompt generation contract: (1) Load Ash prompt from agents/{category}/{role}.md for built-in (or via agent_search MCP for registry/user agents), (2) Use wrapper template from custom-ashes.md for custom Ashes, (3) Inject inscription contract (file list read from tmp/reviews/{identifier}/changed-files.txt), (4) Include talisman config overrides. Parallel execution: summon ALL selected Ash in a single message. Built-in Ashes: prompt loaded from agent definition files. Custom Ashes: agent-backed resolve agent file, CLI-backed invoke external binary with prompt template. Security: SEC-006 — file list written to changed-files.txt, NOT embedded raw paths in prompts to prevent injection. CLI-backed Ashes have 4-step Hallucination Guard. Nonce-bounded content injection for diffs.
expectations: Describes prompt loading from agents/{category}/{role}.md or via MCP agent_search
Notes custom Ashes use wrapper template
Explains parallel execution: all Ashes summoned in single message
Describes inscription contract injection
Mentions file list written to changed-files.txt (SEC-006)
Notes raw paths NOT embedded in prompts for security
Describes CLI-backed Ash 4-step Hallucination Guard
Mentions nonce-bounded content injection

evals.prompt: Explain Phase 4 (Monitor) and the polling loop pattern. What are the polling parameters? How is stale detection handled? What happens when an Ash times out?
evals.expected_output: Phase 4 Monitor: Polling parameters: POLL_INTERVAL = 30 seconds, MAX_ITERATIONS = 20 (ceil(600000/30000) = 10 min timeout), STALE_WARN = 300000 (5 min). Correct pattern: TaskList() → count completed → check stale/timeout → Bash('sleep 30') → repeat. Stale detection: any task in_progress > 5 min logs warning, no auto-release (review findings are non-fungible). Ash timeout (>5 min): proceed with partial results, report gap in TOME.md. Total timeout (>10 min): final sweep, collect partial results, report incomplete. The polling-guard skill provides background knowledge, and POLL-001 hook blocks sleep+echo anti-patterns.
expectations: States POLL_INTERVAL = 30 seconds
States MAX_ITERATIONS = 20 (10 min timeout)
States STALE_WARN = 300000 (5 min)
Describes correct pattern: TaskList() → count → check → sleep → repeat
Notes stale detection logs warning at 5 min, no auto-release
Explains Ash timeout: proceed with partial results, report gap
Explains total timeout: final sweep, collect partial, report incomplete
Mentions POLL-001 hook blocks sleep+echo anti-patterns

evals.prompt: Walk me through Phase 5 (Runebinder Aggregation) for /rune:appraise. What is the deduplication hierarchy? How are findings formatted for mend parsing? What is the TOME.md structure?
evals.expected_output: Phase 5 Runebinder: Deduplication hierarchy: SEC > BACK > VEIL > DOUBT > DOC > QUAL > FRONT > CDX > external model prefixes. Finding format: every finding MUST be wrapped in <!-- RUNE:FINDING ... --> markers for mend parsing. TOME.md structure: Executive Summary, P1 Critical Findings, P2 Important Findings, P3 Minor Findings, Per-File Summary, Aggregation Metadata. Phase 5.3 Diff-Scope Tagging: orchestrator tags findings with scope='in-diff' or scope='pre-existing'. Phase 5.5 Cross-Model Verification: only if Codex Oracle was summoned, verifies CDX findings, removes HALLUCINATED + UNVERIFIED findings. Phase 6 Truthsight: Layer 0 inline checks + Layer 2 verifier for P1 findings.
expectations: Lists deduplication hierarchy: SEC > BACK > VEIL > DOUBT > DOC > QUAL > FRONT > CDX > external
Notes findings must be wrapped in <!-- RUNE:FINDING ... --> markers
Describes TOME.md structure: Executive Summary, P1/P2/P3 Findings, Per-File Summary, Metadata
Explains Phase 5.3 scope tagging: in-diff vs pre-existing
Describes Phase 5.5 Codex verification for CDX findings
Notes removal of HALLUCINATED + UNVERIFIED findings
Mentions Phase 6 Truthsight: Layer 0 + Layer 2 for P1

evals.prompt: Explain Phase 7 (Cleanup & Echo Persist) for /rune:appraise. What are the 5 components of the standard cleanup pattern? What is the CHOME pattern? What gets persisted to Echoes?
evals.expected_output: Phase 7 Cleanup: 5 components: (1) Dynamic member discovery — read team config for all teammates; (2) shutdown_request to all members via SendMessage; (3) Grace period — 20 seconds for deregistration; (4) TeamDelete with retry-with-backoff (4 attempts: 0s, 5s, 10s, 15s); (5) Filesystem fallback — only if TeamDelete never succeeded (QUAL-012). CHOME pattern: CHOME='${CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR:-$HOME/.claude}' — never hardcode ~/.claude/ in Bash cleanup. Echo persist: P1/P2 patterns with recurrence potential are persisted to .claude/echoes/{role}/MEMORY.md. Workflow lock released. State file updated to completed. Auto-mend invocation if --auto-mend and P1/P2 findings exist.
expectations: Lists 5 cleanup components in order
Notes grace period is 20 seconds
Lists TeamDelete retry delays: 0s, 5s, 10s, 15s
Explains QUAL-012: gate filesystem fallback behind !cleanupTeamDeleteSucceeded
Describes CHOME pattern for multi-account support
Notes P1/P2 patterns persisted to Echoes
Mentions workflow lock release
Notes auto-mend option with --auto-mend flag

evals.prompt: What are all the flag interactions for /rune:appraise? Explain what happens with: --deep + --partial, --deep + --cycles N, --deep + --max-agents N, --deep + --no-converge, --cycles N standalone.
evals.expected_output: Flag interactions: --deep + --partial: Warning about sparse findings from investigation Ashes, proceeds (not hard error). --deep + --cycles N (N>1): Warning about N x 3 waves = up to {N*18} agent invocations, proceeds with cost warning. --deep + --max-agents N: Applies to Wave 1 only, Wave 2/3 are deepOnly and not subject to cap. --deep + --no-converge: Deep waves still execute, --no-converge affects per-chunk convergence not wave scheduling. --cycles N standalone: Run N standalone review passes with TOME merge between passes, no deep waves, useful for catching issues that require multiple passes.
expectations: Describes --deep + --partial: warning, proceeds
Describes --deep + --cycles N: warning about N*18 agents
Describes --deep + --max-agents: Wave 1 only
Describes --deep + --no-converge: affects chunk convergence, not waves
Describes --cycles N standalone: N passes with TOME merge
Notes all interactions are non-blocking warnings

evals.prompt: Explain the error handling matrix for /rune:appraise. What happens when: (1) Ash timeout (>5 min), (2) Total timeout (>10 min), (3) Ash crash, (4) ALL Ash fail, (5) Concurrent review running, (6) Codex CLI not installed, (7) Codex not authenticated.
evals.expected_output: Error handling: (1) Ash timeout (>5 min): proceed with partial results, log gap, report in TOME. (2) Total timeout (>10 min): final sweep, collect partial results, report incomplete. (3) Ash crash: report gap in TOME.md. (4) ALL Ash fail: abort, notify user. (5) Concurrent review: warn, offer to cancel previous workflow. (6) Codex CLI not installed: skip Codex Oracle, log 'CLI not found'. (7) Codex not authenticated: skip Codex Oracle, log 'run codex login'. (8) Codex exec timeout (>10 min): Codex Oracle partial results, log 'timeout — reduce context_budget'. (9) jq unavailable: Codex Oracle uses raw text fallback instead of JSONL parsing. All errors are non-blocking where possible.
expectations: Ash timeout: proceed with partial, log gap
Total timeout: final sweep, partial results, report incomplete
Ash crash: report gap in TOME.md
ALL Ash fail: abort, notify user
Concurrent review: warn, offer cancel
Codex not installed: skip Oracle, log message
Codex not authenticated: skip Oracle, suggest login
Codex timeout: partial results, suggest reduce budget
jq unavailable: raw text fallback

evals.prompt: How does Phase 0.5 Lore Layer work in /rune:appraise? When is it skipped? What does lore-analyst output? How does it affect file ordering?
evals.expected_output: Phase 0.5 Lore Layer: Purpose: git history risk scoring runs BEFORE team creation. lore-analyst agent (bare Agent, no team yet — ATE-1 exemption) outputs risk-map.json and lore-analysis.md. Risk tiers: CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, STALE. File re-sorting: changed_files re-sorted by risk tier (CRITICAL → HIGH → MEDIUM → LOW → STALE). Skip conditions: non-git repo, --no-lore flag, talisman.goldmask.layers.lore.enabled === false, fewer than 5 commits in lookback window (G5 guard). The risk scores are used for prioritized review ordering and injected into inscription.json.
expectations: Notes lore-analyst runs as bare Agent (ATE-1 exemption)
Lists outputs: risk-map.json, lore-analysis.md
Lists risk tiers: CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, STALE
Describes file re-sorting by risk tier
Lists skip conditions: non-git, --no-lore, lore.enabled === false, <5 commits (G5 guard)
Notes risk scores injected into inscription.json

evals.prompt: Explain Phase 0.3 Context Intelligence and Phase 0.4 Linter Detection for /rune:appraise. What metadata is gathered? How does linter awareness prevent duplicate findings?
evals.expected_output: Phase 0.3 Context Intelligence: Gathers PR metadata and linked issue context via gh CLI. Injects contextIntel into inscription.json. Includes sanitizeUntrustedText() for CDX-001/CVE-2021-42574 protection (Unicode bidi character stripping). Skipped when no gh CLI, --partial, or disabled in talisman. Phase 0.4 Linter Detection: Discovers project linters (eslint, prettier, ruff, clippy, etc.) via manifest scanning. Injects linter_context into inscription.json. Linter awareness suppresses duplicate findings — Ash skip reporting issues that linters would catch. SEC-*/VEIL-* findings are NEVER suppressed (security always reported). Configurable via talisman.review.linter_awareness.
expectations: Describes Phase 0.3: PR metadata, linked issues via gh CLI
Mentions sanitizeUntrustedText() for Unicode bidi protection
Notes skip conditions: no gh CLI, --partial, disabled
Describes Phase 0.4: linter discovery via manifest scanning
Lists example linters: eslint, prettier, ruff, clippy
Notes linter awareness suppresses duplicate findings
Mentions SEC-*/VEIL-* findings are NEVER suppressed
Notes configurable via talisman.review.linter_awareness

evals.prompt: How does the Workflow Lock work in /rune:appraise? What lock type is used? What happens when there's a conflict? How is the lock released?
evals.expected_output: Workflow Lock: Lock type: 'reader' (appraise reads repository state, doesn't modify files). Conflict detection: rune_check_conflicts('reader') checks for active writers (strive, mend, arc work phases). On conflict: AskUserQuestion prompts user to proceed anyway or wait. Lock acquisition: rune_acquire_lock('appraise', 'reader') creates lock file. Lock file location: tmp/.rune-lock-appraise-{identifier}.json. Lock release: Phase 7 cleanup releases lock via rm -f. Lock file includes: config_dir, owner_pid, session_id, workflow, lock_type, acquired_at. Session isolation: hooks verify owner_pid matches $PPID before acting on lock files.
expectations: States lock type is 'reader'
Notes conflict detection checks for active writers
Describes AskUserQuestion prompt on conflict
Names lock acquisition function: rune_acquire_lock
Notes lock release in Phase 7 cleanup
Lists lock file contents: config_dir, owner_pid, session_id, workflow, lock_type, acquired_at
Notes session isolation via owner_pid verification

evals.prompt: Explain the chunk routing logic for /rune:appraise. When does chunking trigger? What is CHUNK_THRESHOLD? How does the chunk lifecycle work?
evals.expected_output: Chunk routing: CHUNK_THRESHOLD = 20 files (default, configurable via --chunk-size N). Trigger: changed_files.length > CHUNK_THRESHOLD AND --no-chunk NOT set. Chunk lifecycle: (1) Split files into chunks of CHUNK_SIZE (default 20), (2) Each chunk runs full Roundtable Circle pass, (3) TOMEs merged between chunks, (4) Convergence loop runs after all chunks complete (unless --no-converge). Convergence: re-review files with P1/P2 findings from previous pass, up to MAX_CONVERGENCE_CYCLES. --no-chunk: forces single-pass review regardless of file count. --cycles N: alternative to chunking, runs N standalone passes with TOME merge.
expectations: States CHUNK_THRESHOLD = 20 files (default)
Notes configurable via --chunk-size N
Describes trigger: file count > threshold AND --no-chunk not set
Describes chunk lifecycle: split → pass per chunk → TOME merge → convergence
Mentions --no-chunk forces single-pass
Mentions --cycles N as alternative multi-pass approach
Notes convergence re-reviews P1/P2 files

evals.prompt: What is Phase 4.5 Doubt Seer in /rune:appraise? When does it run? What is the verdict format? How does BLOCK verdict affect the workflow?
evals.expected_output: Phase 4.5 Doubt Seer: CONDITIONAL — strict opt-in via talisman.doubt_seer.enabled = true. Purpose: cross-examines P1/P2 findings with adversarial questioning. Timeout: 5 minutes. Output: VERDICT field in doubt-seer.md. Verdicts: BLOCK (unproven P1 findings detected, sets workflow_blocked flag), CONCERN (unproven claims exist but workflow continues), PASS (all findings have supporting evidence). BLOCK verdict: when block_on_unproven: true in talisman, halts workflow and requires user intervention. Spawned as bare Agent (no team context) for cross-examination.
expectations: Notes strict opt-in: talisman.doubt_seer.enabled = true
States purpose: cross-examine P1/P2 findings
Notes 5 minute timeout
Lists verdicts: BLOCK, CONCERN, PASS
Describes BLOCK verdict: sets workflow_blocked flag
Notes spawned as bare Agent (no team context)
Mentions block_on_unproven talisman setting

evals.prompt: Explain the --dry-run flag behavior for /rune:appraise. What phases execute? What is displayed? What is NOT created?
evals.expected_output: --dry-run behavior: Executes Phase 0 (Pre-flight) and Phase 1 (Rune Gaze) only. Displays: changed files classified by type, which Ash would be summoned, file assignments per Ash, estimated team size, chunk plan if file count exceeds CHUNK_THRESHOLD, warnings about sparse findings (if --deep + --partial). Does NOT create: teams (TeamCreate), tasks (TaskCreate), state files, inscription.json, signal directories, agents (Agent tool). Exits after display with summary. Useful for previewing review scope before committing to full execution.
expectations: States executes Phase 0 and Phase 1 only
Lists displayed info: files, Ash plan, assignments, team size, chunk plan
Notes --deep + --partial warning shown
Lists NOT created: teams, tasks, state files, inscription.json, signals, agents
Notes exits after display with summary
Describes use case: preview scope before full execution

evals.prompt: How does custom Ash discovery work in /rune:appraise? Explain the difference between agent-backed and CLI-backed custom Ashes. How are they discovered in Phase 1? What security validations apply?
evals.expected_output: Custom Ash discovery (Phase 1 Rune Gaze): Agent-backed: defined in talisman.yml → ashes.custom[] with 'agent' field pointing to .claude/agents/{name}.md. Discovered by matching 'triggers' against changed_files. Resolved by source priority: local > global > plugin. CLI-backed: defined with 'cli:' field (discriminated union) specifying binary path, model name, and arguments. Validated against CLI_BINARY_PATTERN (alphanumeric, dash, underscore), MODEL_NAME_PATTERN, and CLI_PATH_VALIDATION (must NOT resolve within project directory). Subject to max_cli_ashes sub-cap (default: 2) within max_ashes. Detection via detectExternalModel() generalization of Codex detection.
expectations: Describes agent-backed: talisman.yml ashes.custom[] with agent field
Describes CLI-backed: talisman.yml with cli: field
Notes source priority: local > global > plugin
Mentions triggers matching against changed_files
Lists CLI security: CLI_BINARY_PATTERN, MODEL_NAME_PATTERN, CLI_PATH_VALIDATION
Notes max_cli_ashes sub-cap (default: 2)
References detectExternalModel() for detection

evals.prompt: Explain Codex Oracle integration in /rune:appraise. When is it summoned? What are the detection steps? How does Phase 5.5 verification work? What error handling applies?
evals.expected_output: Codex Oracle integration: Summoned when: (1) codex CLI available, (2) authenticated via 'codex login', (3) not disabled in talisman.codex.enabled, (4) context_budget allows. Detection: check 'codex' in PATH, verify auth status. Execution: 'codex exec' with prompt from external-model-template.md, diff/file content via nonce-bounded injection. Hallucination Guard 4-step: (1) diff relevance check, (2) file existence verification, (3) line range validation, (4) cross-reference with Claude findings. Phase 5.5 verification: removes HALLUCINATED and UNVERIFIED CDX findings. Error handling: CLI not found → skip with log; not authenticated → log 'run codex login'; timeout → partial results, suggest reduce budget; jq missing → raw text fallback.
expectations: Lists summon conditions: CLI available, authenticated, not disabled, budget allows
Describes detection: PATH check, auth verification
Notes execution via 'codex exec' with external-model-template.md
Describes 4-step Hallucination Guard
Explains Phase 5.5 removes HALLUCINATED/UNVERIFIED findings
Lists error handling: CLI missing, auth, timeout, jq fallback

evals.prompt: What is the inscription contract in /rune:appraise? What data is injected into agent prompts? How does it enable output validation?
evals.expected_output: Inscription contract: Generated in Phase 2 (Forge Team), injected into all Ash prompts in Phase 3. Contents: (1) team_name — for Agent Teams coordination; (2) output_dir — where Ash writes findings; (3) diff_scope — file paths with line ranges from git diff; (4) context_intelligence — PR metadata, linked issues from Phase 0.3; (5) linter_context — discovered linters from Phase 0.4; (6) risk_map — from Phase 0.5 Lore Layer; (7) verification settings — for Phase 5.5 cross-model verification. Output validation: expected_files array lists each Ash output path, enabling Phase 4 Monitor to track completion and Phase 7 Cleanup to verify all expected outputs exist. Written to tmp/reviews/{identifier}/inscription.json.
expectations: Notes inscription generated in Phase 2, injected in Phase 3
Lists contents: team_name, output_dir, diff_scope, context_intelligence, linter_context, risk_map, verification
Explains diff_scope contains file paths with line ranges
Notes context_intelligence from Phase 0.3
Describes output validation via expected_files array
Notes file location: tmp/reviews/{identifier}/inscription.json

evals.prompt: Explain the TOME.md finding format in /rune:appraise. What are the finding prefixes? How is severity determined? What is the wrap format for mend parsing?
evals.expected_output: TOME.md finding format: Prefixes by Ash role — SEC (Ward Sentinel security), BACK (Forge Warden backend), FRONT (Glyph Scribe frontend), VEIL (Veil Piercer truthbinding), DOC (Knowledge Keeper documentation), QUAL (Pattern Weaver quality), CDX (Codex Oracle cross-model), UXH/UXF/UXI/UXC (UX reviewers). DOUBT prefix for Doubt Seer meta-findings (exempt from dedup). Severity levels: P1 (Critical — security vulnerabilities, data loss risk), P2 (Important — bugs, performance issues), P3 (Minor — style, suggestions). Wrap format: <!-- RUNE:FINDING nonce="..." id="SEC-001" file="..." line="42" severity="P1" --> followed by finding content, then <!-- /RUNE:FINDING id="SEC-001" -->. Required attributes: nonce, id, file, line, severity. The nonce prevents cross-session injection attacks.
expectations: Lists prefixes: SEC, BACK, FRONT, VEIL, DOC, QUAL, CDX, UXH/UXF/UXI/UXC
Notes DOUBT prefix exempt from dedup
Describes severity: P1 Critical, P2 Important, P3 Minor
Shows wrap format with RUNE:FINDING markers
Lists required attributes: nonce, id, file, line, severity
Notes nonce prevents cross-session injection

evals.prompt: How does session isolation work in /rune:appraise? What mechanisms prevent cross-session interference? How do hooks verify ownership?
evals.expected_output: Session isolation mechanisms: (1) State files include config_dir (CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR path), owner_pid ($PPID), session_id (CLAUDE_SESSION_ID) — stored in tmp/.rune-review-{identifier}.json. (2) Team names include gitHash and shortSession for uniqueness. (3) Hook scripts verify ownership before acting: check config_dir matches current CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR, check owner_pid matches $PPID (with kill -0 liveness check). (4) Stale detection: if owner_pid is dead, orphan recovery cleans up. (5) Lock files include same isolation fields. Cross-session safety: different sessions working on same repo see each other's state files but skip them due to ownership mismatch. The resolve-session-identity.sh script provides RUNE_CURRENT_CFG for consistent comparison.
expectations: Lists state file fields: config_dir, owner_pid, session_id
Notes team names include gitHash and shortSession
Describes hook ownership verification
Mentions kill -0 liveness check for PID
Explains orphan recovery when owner_pid is dead
Notes lock files include same isolation fields
References resolve-session-identity.sh for RUNE_CURRENT_CFG

evals.prompt: Explain the team transition protocol in /rune:appraise Phase 2. What is the pre-create guard? How does TeamDelete retry-with-backoff work? What is the filesystem fallback?
evals.expected_output: Team transition protocol (Phase 2): Pre-create guard ensures clean state before TeamCreate. Steps: (1) Validate identifier matches /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/ (security); (2) TeamDelete with retry-with-backoff — 3 attempts with delays [0s, 3s, 8s] to handle transient failures; (3) If TeamDelete fails all attempts, check if team directory exists; (4) Filesystem fallback: rm -rf the team directory only if TeamDelete never succeeded (QUAL-012 gate prevents unnecessary rm -rf); (5) TeamCreate with catch-and-recover for 'Already leading' error (idempotent); (6) Post-create verification: read config.json to confirm team exists. The CHOME pattern ensures correct config directory resolution: CHOME='${CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR:-$HOME/.claude}' in Bash commands.
expectations: Lists pre-create guard steps
Notes identifier validation: /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/
Describes TeamDelete retry: 3 attempts, delays [0s, 3s, 8s]
Explains QUAL-012: gate filesystem fallback behind !TeamDeleteSucceeded
Notes 'Already leading' catch-and-recover for idempotency
Mentions post-create verification via config.json read
References CHOME pattern for config directory

evals.prompt: How does the convergence loop work in /rune:appraise? What quality gates trigger re-review? What are the convergence states?
evals.expected_output: Convergence loop: Runs after all chunks complete (unless --no-converge). Quality gates: (1) P1/P2 findings exist that could be resolved by re-review; (2) Confidence scores below threshold suggest missed issues; (3) Coverage gaps indicate incomplete Ash output. Convergence states: CONVERGED (no new findings, quality acceptable), HALTED (max cycles reached without convergence), RETRY (issues found that warrant re-review). Process: (1) Identify files with P1/P2 findings, (2) Re-run review on subset, (3) Compare new vs old findings, (4) If delta < threshold, mark CONVERGED. MAX_CONVERGENCE_CYCLES prevents infinite loops. Each convergence pass gets reduced timeout (50% of initial). --no-converge flag disables this loop entirely.
expectations: Notes runs after all chunks complete unless --no-converge
Lists quality gate triggers: P1/P2 findings, low confidence, coverage gaps
Describes convergence states: CONVERGED, HALTED, RETRY
Explains re-review on files with P1/P2 findings
Notes MAX_CONVERGENCE_CYCLES prevents infinite loops
Mentions reduced timeout per pass (50%)
Notes --no-converge disables loop

evals.prompt: Explain Phase 1.5 UX Reviewer Selection in /rune:appraise. When are UX reviewers added? What UX agents are available? How does --deep affect UX selection?
evals.expected_output: Phase 1.5 UX Reviewer Selection: Gated by talisman.ux.enabled === true AND frontend files detected in changed_files (extensions: .tsx, .jsx, .vue, .svelte, .css, .scss). Default UX agent: ux-heuristic-reviewer (UXH-prefixed findings, non-blocking). With --deep flag: adds ux-flow-validator (UXF-prefixed, validates loading/error/empty states), ux-interaction-auditor (UXI-prefixed, checks hover/focus/touch targets). Optional (talisman.ux.cognitive_walkthrough === true): ux-cognitive-walker (UXC-prefixed, expensive opus model, simulates first-time user). Skip conditions: talisman.ux.enabled not true, or no frontend files in diff. UX findings are non-blocking by default — they inform but don't block workflows.
expectations: Notes gate: talisman.ux.enabled AND frontend files
Lists frontend extensions: .tsx, .jsx, .vue, .svelte, .css, .scss
Describes default: ux-heuristic-reviewer (UXH prefix, non-blocking)
Notes --deep adds ux-flow-validator and ux-interaction-auditor
Mentions ux-cognitive-walker requires opt-in, uses opus
Notes UX findings are non-blocking by default

evals.prompt: How does the --auto-mend flag work in /rune:appraise? When is mend invoked? What findings trigger it?
evals.expected_output: --auto-mend flag: Automatically invokes /rune:mend after review completes if qualifying findings exist. Trigger conditions: (1) TOME.md exists, (2) P1 (Critical) or P2 (Important) findings present. Process: (1) Phase 7 Cleanup completes normally, (2) Check TOME.md for P1/P2 severity findings, (3) If found, invoke /rune:mend tmp/reviews/{identifier}/TOME.md, (4) Mend runs in parallel with one fixer per file group. Findings that trigger: SEC-* (security vulnerabilities), BACK-* critical bugs, VEIL-* truthbinding violations. Does NOT trigger for: P3 (Minor) findings only, DOC-* (documentation), UXH-* (UX heuristic, non-blocking). User can also run mend manually after reviewing TOME.md.
expectations: Notes automatic /rune:mend invocation after review
Lists trigger conditions: TOME.md exists, P1/P2 findings present
Describes process after Phase 7 cleanup
Notes one fixer per file group in mend
Lists triggering findings: SEC-*, BACK-* critical, VEIL-*
Notes P3 only does NOT trigger auto-mend
Mentions manual mend option after reviewing TOME

evals.prompt: Explain signal-based completion detection in /rune:appraise. How does the TaskCompleted hook work? What signal files are written?
evals.expected_output: Signal-based completion detection: Enables fast (5-second) detection of Ash completion without polling TaskList. Setup (Phase 2): Create signal directory tmp/.rune-signals/rune-review-{identifier}/, write .expected file with count of Ashes. TaskCompleted hook: Triggered when Ash marks task complete. Script on-task-completed.sh: (1) Writes signal file named after task_id to signal directory, (2) Runs haiku quality gate to validate completion legitimacy. Signal file format: {task_id}.signal containing completion timestamp. Monitor (Phase 4): Can check signal directory for completion files as alternative to TaskList polling. The SEAL convention: Ashes emit <seal>TAG</seal> as last line of output for deterministic completion detection. Hook verifies expected file was written before allowing idle.

Notes haiku quality gate validates completion
Describes signal file format: {task_id}.signal
Mentions SEAL convention for deterministic detection
Notes hook verifies expected file before idle

evals.prompt: Explain the CLI-backed Ash Hallucination Guard in detail. What are the 4 steps? What is Step 0? What happens when a finding fails verification?
evals.expected_output: Hallucination Guard 4-step verification for CLI-backed Ashes: Step 0 (diff relevance check) — verifies the finding references code that was actually changed. Step 1 (file existence) — confirms the file path exists in the repository. Step 2 (line range validation) — checks that referenced line numbers fall within actual file bounds. Step 3 (cross-reference) — compares finding against Claude Ash output for consensus. When a finding fails verification: tagged as HALLUCINATED or UNVERIFIED, removed in Phase 5.5 cross-model verification step. Purpose: prevent external models from generating plausible-sounding but fictitious findings. Nonce-bounded content injection prevents cross-session contamination.
expectations: Describes Step 0: diff relevance check
Describes Step 1: file existence verification
Describes Step 2: line range validation
Describes Step 3: cross-reference with Claude findings
Notes failed findings tagged as HALLUCINATED or UNVERIFIED
Mentions removal in Phase 5.5
Notes nonce-bounded content injection prevents cross-session contamination

evals.prompt: How does Phase 1 handle file classification for mixed-stack projects? What happens with monorepos containing Python backend + TypeScript frontend? What about files with unknown extensions?
evals.expected_output: Mixed-stack file classification in Phase 1: Monorepo with Python backend + TypeScript frontend triggers BOTH Forge Warden (backend) and Glyph Scribe (frontend) Ashes. Files are assigned to the appropriate Ash based on extension. Backend files (.py, .go, .rs, .php) → Forge Warden. Frontend files (.tsx, .jsx, .vue, .svelte, .css, .scss) → Glyph Scribe. Config files (.yml, .yaml, .json, .toml) → Pattern Weaver quality review. Unknown extensions: classified as 'general', reviewed by Pattern Weaver + Ward Sentinel + Veil Piercer (always-on Ashes). The classification table in rune-gaze.md provides the full mapping. Each file can match multiple reviewers (e.g., .py matches Forge Warden AND always-on Ashes).
expectations: Notes Python + TypeScript monorepo triggers both Forge Warden and Glyph Scribe
Lists backend extensions: .py, .go, .rs, .php
Lists frontend extensions: .tsx, .jsx, .vue, .svelte, .css, .scss
Notes config files → Pattern Weaver
Describes unknown extensions as 'general' reviewed by always-on Ashes
Mentions each file can match multiple reviewers

evals.prompt: Explain finding deduplication in Phase 5 Runebinder. How are duplicate findings detected? What is the prefix hierarchy? How are findings with different severity levels handled?
evals.expected_output: Finding deduplication in Phase 5: Duplicate detection uses 5-line proximity window — findings within 5 lines of each other referencing the same issue are candidates for dedup. Prefix hierarchy (highest wins): SEC > BACK > VEIL > DOUBT > DOC > QUAL > FRONT > CDX > external. When findings overlap: higher-priority prefix wins, severity is max() of all duplicates. Example: if Ward Sentinel reports SEC-001 P2 and Forge Warden reports BACK-042 P1 for the same issue → SEC-001 P1 (SEC wins, max severity). DOUBT prefix is exempt from dedup — Doubt Seer meta-findings are always preserved. The dedup step runs BEFORE TOME.md generation to ensure clean output for mend parsing.
expectations: Notes 5-line proximity window for duplicate detection
Lists prefix hierarchy: SEC > BACK > VEIL > DOUBT > DOC > QUAL > FRONT > CDX > external
Explains higher prefix wins on overlap
Notes severity is max() of duplicates
Gives example: SEC P2 + BACK P1 → SEC P1
Notes DOUBT prefix exempt from dedup
Notes dedup runs BEFORE TOME.md generation

evals.prompt: How does Phase 5.3 diff-scope tagging work? What determines if a finding is 'in-diff' vs 'pre-existing'? Why does this matter for mend priority?
evals.expected_output: Phase 5.3 diff-scope tagging: Determined by whether the finding's file:line falls within the git diff ranges from Phase 0. In-diff: finding location is within changed lines (added or modified). Pre-existing: finding location is in unchanged lines (file was touched but line wasn't modified). The diff_scope data from inscription.json contains the line ranges. Why it matters for mend: in-diff findings are HIGH priority (user's changes caused them), pre-existing findings are LOWER priority (existing tech debt, not introduced by this PR). The scope attribute is added to each finding's RUNE:FINDING marker. Pre-existing findings may be deferred to a separate tech debt ticket rather than blocking the PR.
expectations: Explains in-diff: finding within changed lines
Explains pre-existing: finding in unchanged lines of touched file
Notes diff_scope from inscription.json contains line ranges
Describes mend priority: in-diff HIGH, pre-existing LOWER
Notes scope attribute in RUNE:FINDING marker
Mentions pre-existing may be deferred to tech debt ticket

evals.prompt: Explain Phase 6 Truthsight verification. What is Layer 0 vs Layer 2? When does each apply? What triggers Layer 2 verification?
evals.expected_output: Phase 6 Truthsight verification: Layer 0 (inline checks) — quick sanity checks run inline by Runebinder: (1) File exists at path, (2) Line number within bounds, (3) No contradictory findings. Runs on ALL findings. Layer 2 (verifier agent) — deep verification by dedicated agent. Triggers: (1) P1 severity findings, (2) Layer 0 flagged as suspicious, (3) CDX findings from Codex Oracle. Layer 2 spawns a haiku-model verifier agent that re-reads the source file and confirms the finding's accuracy. If Layer 2 fails: finding is tagged as UNVERIFIED and removed from final TOME. Layer 1 is intentionally skipped (reserved for future use). The two-layer approach balances speed (Layer 0 on everything) with depth (Layer 2 on critical).
expectations: Describes Layer 0: inline sanity checks on ALL findings
Lists Layer 0 checks: file exists, line in bounds, no contradictions
Describes Layer 2: deep verification by agent
Lists Layer 2 triggers: P1 findings, Layer 0 suspicious, CDX findings
Notes Layer 2 spawns haiku-model verifier
Mentions UNVERIFIED findings removed from TOME
Notes Layer 1 is intentionally skipped

evals.prompt: How does the Glyph Budget work for inter-agent communication? What is the limit? What happens when exceeded?
evals.expected_output: Glyph Budget for inter-agent communication: Default limit is 300 words per SendMessage. Enforced by the enforce-glyph-budget.sh PostToolUse hook. When exceeded: advisory context injected (non-blocking), warns teammate to compress their message. The budget applies to SendMessage tool only — not to agent output files. Purpose: prevent context explosion when teammates send verbose messages to each other. Compression strategies: (1) Use bullet points, (2) Reference file paths instead of quoting code, (3) Summarize instead of full transcripts. Configurable via talisman.yml context_weaving.glyph_budget. The hook counts words using simple whitespace splitting. Not a hard limit — advisory to encourage brevity.
expectations: States default limit: 300 words per SendMessage
Notes enforced by enforce-glyph-budget.sh PostToolUse hook
Describes behavior: advisory context injected (non-blocking)
Lists compression strategies: bullets, file paths, summaries
Notes configurable via talisman context_weaving.glyph_budget
Clarifies applies to SendMessage only, not output files

evals.prompt: Explain the ATE-1 hook enforcement. When are bare Agent() calls blocked? What are the 4 detection signals? What agents are exempt?
evals.expected_output: ATE-1 hook enforcement: Blocks bare Agent() calls (without team_name) during active Rune workflows. 4 detection signals: (1) tmp/.rune-*-*.json state files, (2) inscription.json in output directories, (3) tmp/.rune-signals/ directories, (4) agent names matching known Rune patterns. Exemptions: (1) Non-Rune agents (names not in lib/known-rune-agents.sh), (2) Explore/Plan agent types, (3) Phase 0.5 lore-analyst (runs before team exists, ATE-1 exemption documented). The hook filters by session ownership (config_dir + owner_pid match). Unnamed agents (no name parameter) are always blocked. Purpose: ensure all agents participate in the team coordination protocol for proper cleanup and monitoring.
expectations: States blocks bare Agent() calls without team_name
Lists 4 signals: state files, inscription.json, signal dirs, name patterns
Notes exemptions: non-Rune agents, Explore/Plan types, lore-analyst
Notes session ownership filtering: config_dir + owner_pid
Mentions unnamed agents always blocked
Explains purpose: team coordination for cleanup/monitoring

evals.prompt: What is the max_cli_ashes limit? How does it interact with max_ashes? What security validations apply to CLI binary paths?
evals.expected_output: max_cli_ashes limit: Default is 2. This is a sub-cap within max_ashes — you can have at most 2 CLI-backed Ashes even if max_ashes is higher. Purpose: limit external model exposure (higher hallucination risk). Security validations for CLI binary paths: (1) CLI_BINARY_PATTERN — must match ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$ (alphanumeric, dash, underscore only), (2) MODEL_NAME_PATTERN — validates model identifier format, (3) CLI_PATH_VALIDATION — binary path must NOT resolve within project directory (prevents executing project-local binaries). Example: './local-llama' would be rejected, '/usr/local/bin/gemini' would be allowed. These patterns are defined in roundtable-circle/references/security-patterns.md. Codex Oracle is NOT counted toward max_cli_ashes — it has its own dedicated gate.
expectations: States max_cli_ashes default: 2
Notes it's a sub-cap within max_ashes
Explains purpose: limit external model hallucination risk
Lists CLI_BINARY_PATTERN: alphanumeric, dash, underscore
Lists CLI_PATH_VALIDATION: must not resolve within project
Gives example: './local-llama' rejected, '/usr/local/bin/gemini' allowed
Notes Codex Oracle not counted toward limit

evals.prompt: Explain the SEAL convention for Ash completion. What is the format? How does it enable deterministic detection? What hook validates it?
evals.expected_output: SEAL convention for Ash completion: Format is <seal>TAG</seal> as the LAST line of agent output. TAG identifies the Ash (e.g., 'ward-sentinel', 'forge-warden'). Enables deterministic completion detection: the on-teammate-idle.sh hook scans for SEAL marker before allowing idle transition. If missing, the hook blocks idle and logs a warning. This ensures Ashes don't go idle prematurely before writing their output file. The SEAL marker is separate from the signal file system — it's a content-based check that the agent actually completed its work. Combined with signal files (Phase 2 setup), provides two-layer completion verification. The SEAL must be the exact last line — trailing whitespace or newlines invalidate it.
expectations: States format: <seal>TAG</seal> as last line
Notes TAG identifies the Ash
Describes hook: on-teammate-idle.sh validates before idle
Notes missing SEAL blocks idle with warning
Explains purpose: prevent premature idle before output written
Mentions two-layer verification with signal files
Notes SEAL must be exact last line, no trailing whitespace

evals.prompt: How does the Inner Flame self-review protocol work for Ashes? What are the 3 layers? How is it enforced?
evals.expected_output: Inner Flame self-review protocol for Ashes: 3 layers — (1) Grounding: verify all claims reference actual code from the reviewed files, not hallucinated content. (2) Completeness: confirm all assigned files were reviewed, no gaps. (3) Self-Adversarial: challenge own findings — would a skeptic accept this evidence? Enforcement: validate-inner-flame.sh TaskCompleted hook checks output for Grounding/Completeness/Self-Adversarial markers. Missing markers → advisory warning (non-blocking for review Ashes). Configurable via talisman.inner_flame.enabled. The protocol is adapted per agent role: review Ashes focus on evidence quality, work Ashes focus on implementation correctness, fixer Ashes focus on change scope. The hook uses pattern matching to detect marker presence, not semantic validation.
expectations: Lists 3 layers: Grounding, Completeness, Self-Adversarial
Describes Grounding: claims reference actual code
Describes Completeness: all assigned files reviewed
Describes Self-Adversarial: challenge own findings
Notes hook: validate-inner-flame.sh on TaskCompleted
Mentions configurable via talisman.inner_flame.enabled
Notes adapted per agent role

evals.prompt: Explain the sanitizeUntrustedText() function used in Phase 0.3 Context Intelligence. What does it protect against? What characters are stripped?
evals.expected_output: sanitizeUntrustedText() function in Phase 0.3: Protects against CDX-001/CVE-2021-42574 (Unicode bidi character attack). Characters stripped: (1) U+202A-U+202E (LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO bidi overrides), (2) U+2066-U+2069 (LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI isolate controls), (3) U+200E-U+200F (LRM, RLM marks). Purpose: prevent attackers from embedding invisible bidi characters in issue titles/PR descriptions that could cause misleading code display. Applied to all external text before inclusion in inscription.json. The function is defined in phase-0.3-0.4-context-and-linter.md. This is a defense-in-depth measure — even if upstream sources are compromised, the sanitized output cannot contain bidi obfuscation attacks.
expectations: References CDX-001/CVE-2021-42574
Lists bidi override codes: U+202A-U+202E
Lists isolate controls: U+2066-U+2069
Lists directional marks: U+200E-U+200F
Notes applied to external text before inscription.json
Mentions function defined in phase-0.3-0.4-context-and-linter.md

evals.prompt: What happens when Phase 0.4 detects multiple linters for the same file type? How does linter awareness suppress findings? Which finding types are NEVER suppressed?
evals.expected_output: Multiple linters for same file type: Phase 0.4 detects all configured linters, not just one. Example: a TypeScript project may have eslint, prettier, AND typescript compiler checks. All are included in linter_context. Finding suppression logic: when an Ash would report an issue that a configured linter already catches, the finding is suppressed to avoid duplicate noise. NEVER suppressed: SEC-* (security findings always reported regardless of linter), VEIL-* (truthbinding violations always reported), P1 critical findings (safety net — even if linter should catch it, report anyway). Rationale: security issues may slip through linter gaps, and truthbinding violations indicate fundamental problems that require human attention. Configurable via talisman.review.linter_awareness.
expectations: Notes all configured linters detected, not just one
Gives example: TypeScript with eslint + prettier + tsc
Describes suppression: avoid duplicate findings
Lists NEVER suppressed: SEC-*, VEIL-*, P1 critical
Notes configurable via talisman.review.linter_awareness
Explains rationale: security may slip through, truthbinding needs attention

evals.prompt: How does the G5 guard work in Phase 0.5 Lore Layer? What happens when there are fewer than 5 commits? Why is this threshold important?
evals.expected_output: G5 guard in Phase 0.5 Lore Layer: Skips Lore Layer when git history has fewer than 5 commits in the lookback window. Why this threshold: with <5 commits, there's insufficient git history for meaningful risk scoring. Risk patterns (churn, ownership concentration, co-change clustering) require a minimum sample size to be statistically valid. Without G5 guard: every repo would run Lore Layer even on initial commits, producing misleading 'all files LOW risk' results due to lack of historical signal. Implementation: `git log --oneline --since='6 months ago' | wc -l` — if count < 5, skip entire Phase 0.5. The risk-map.json is not generated, and changed_files keeps its original ordering (no re-sort by risk tier).
expectations: States threshold: fewer than 5 commits in lookback
Explains reason: insufficient history for risk scoring
Notes patterns need minimum sample: churn, ownership, co-change
Describes consequence: skip Phase 0.5 entirely
Notes risk-map.json not generated
Notes changed_files keeps original ordering

evals.prompt: Explain how the resolve-session-identity.sh script works. What environment variables does it provide? How is it used by hooks?
evals.expected_output: resolve-session-identity.sh script: Provides RUNE_CURRENT_CFG variable for consistent session identity comparison. Environment variables: (1) RUNE_CURRENT_CFG — resolved CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR or $HOME/.claude fallback, (2) RUNE_CURRENT_PID — $PPID (Claude Code process), (3) RUNE_SESSION_ID — $CLAUDE_SESSION_ID. Used by hooks: (1) PreToolUse hooks compare RUNE_CURRENT_CFG against state file config_dir to verify ownership, (2) Cleanup hooks use RUNE_CURRENT_PID + kill -0 liveness check before acting on stale state, (3) Session isolation logic uses all three to prevent cross-session interference. The script is sourced, not executed — variables are exported to the calling shell. Location: plugins/rune/scripts/lib/resolve-session-identity.sh.

evals.prompt: What is the difference between CONVERGED, HALTED, and RETRY convergence states? When does each occur? What triggers transition between them?
evals.expected_output: Convergence states: CONVERGED — no new findings from re-review, quality metrics acceptable (delta < threshold). Triggers exit from convergence loop. HALTED — max cycles reached without achieving convergence. MAX_CONVERGENCE_CYCLES exceeded, loop terminates with warning. RETRY — new findings detected or quality below threshold. Triggers another re-review pass with reduced timeout (50% per pass). Transitions: Initial review → check quality gates → if gates fail, RETRY → run re-review → if delta < threshold, CONVERGED; else if cycles < MAX, RETRY; else HALTED. Quality gates that trigger RETRY: (1) P1/P2 findings exist, (2) confidence below threshold, (3) coverage gaps. The CONVERGED state is the happy path — findings stabilized and review complete. HALTED indicates diminishing returns — further cycles unlikely to improve quality.
expectations: Defines CONVERGED: no new findings, quality acceptable
Defines HALTED: max cycles reached without convergence
Defines RETRY: new findings or quality below threshold
Describes transition: RETRY → re-review → CONVERGED or HALTED
Lists quality gates: P1/P2 findings, confidence, coverage
Notes reduced timeout: 50% per pass
Notes HALTED indicates diminishing returns

evals.prompt: How does the quality gate in TaskCompleted hook work? What does the haiku model check? What happens when a task is marked complete prematurely?
evals.expected_output: Quality gate in TaskCompleted hook: on-task-completed.sh triggers haiku-model validation. Checks: (1) Output file exists at expected path, (2) File contains structured findings (RUNE:FINDING markers), (3) Findings have required attributes (nonce, id, file, line, severity), (4) No generic/copypaste content patterns. Premature completion: if haiku gate fails, the hook exits 2 (blocking). The task remains in_progress, teammate cannot go idle. Feedback is injected via stderr. This prevents teammates from gaming the system by marking tasks complete without doing the work. The haiku model is used for cost efficiency — it's a lightweight structural check, not deep semantic validation. The hook reads expected_files from inscription.json to know what output to check.
expectations: Notes hook: on-task-completed.sh with haiku validation
Lists checks: output exists, RUNE:FINDING markers, required attrs
Notes check for generic/copypaste patterns
Describes premature completion: exit 2, task stays in_progress
Notes feedback injected via stderr
Mentions haiku used for cost efficiency
Notes expected_files from inscription.json

evals.prompt: Explain the cross-session concurrency model. Which workflow combinations are supported? Which cause conflicts? How does the workflow lock compatibility matrix work?
evals.expected_output: Cross-session concurrency model: Supported combinations — reader + reader (multiple reviews), reader + writer (review while strive/mend runs), planner + writer (devise while strive runs). Conflict combinations — writer + writer (two strive instances, two mends). Compatibility matrix in workflow-lock.sh: READER conflicts with WRITER only; WRITER conflicts with READER and WRITER; PLANNER conflicts with none (planners don't modify files). When conflict detected: AskUserQuestion offers choice: proceed anyway (dangerous) or wait. Each session has its own team (SDK one-team-per-session constraint). Hook scripts scope cleanup by session ownership — TeamDelete only affects current session's team. Workflow locks prevent file-level conflicts; session isolation prevents team-level conflicts.
expectations: Lists supported: reader+reader, reader+writer, planner+writer
Lists conflict: writer+writer
Describes compatibility matrix: READER vs WRITER
Notes PLANNER conflicts with none
Describes conflict handling: AskUserQuestion choice
Notes each session has own team (SDK constraint)
Mentions hook cleanup scoped by session

evals.prompt: What is the expected_files array in inscription.json? How is it used for output validation? What happens when an expected file is missing?
evals.expected_output: expected_files array in inscription.json: Lists all output file paths that each Ash should produce. Format: [{ ash: 'ward-sentinel', path: 'tmp/reviews/{id}/ward-sentinel.md', status: 'pending' }]. Used for output validation: (1) Phase 4 Monitor checks expected files during polling, (2) Phase 7 Cleanup verifies all expected files exist before cleanup, (3) TaskCompleted hook validates file creation before allowing idle. Missing expected file: (1) Monitor logs warning after timeout, (2) TOME.md includes gap report for missing Ash output, (3) Cleanup still proceeds — partial results are collected. The array is generated in Phase 2 based on selectedAsh list. Each Ash knows its expected output path from the inscription contract injected in Phase 3.
expectations: Describes format: ash name, path, status
Notes Phase 4 Monitor checks during polling
Notes Phase 7 Cleanup verifies before cleanup
Notes TaskCompleted hook validates before idle

evals.prompt: How does the codex exec timeout handling work? What is the timeout value? What happens when it's exceeded? How does jq fallback work?
evals.expected_output: codex exec timeout: 10 minutes (600,000 ms). Configured via talisman.codex.timeout_ms. When exceeded: Codex Oracle returns partial results (whatever was generated before timeout). Log message: 'Codex exec timeout — consider reducing context_budget'. The partial results still go through Hallucination Guard verification. jq fallback: Codex outputs JSONL format. If jq is unavailable, Codex Oracle uses raw text parsing — regex-based extraction of findings instead of structured JSON parsing. Less reliable but functional. Detection: `which jq` returns non-zero → enable fallback mode. The fallback mode may miss structured metadata (severity, file path) and relies more heavily on Hallucination Guard to validate extracted findings. Error handling never blocks the review — Codex failure is non-fatal.
expectations: States timeout: 10 minutes (600,000 ms)
Notes configurable via talisman.codex.timeout_ms
Describes timeout: partial results + log message
Notes partial results go through Hallucination Guard
Describes jq fallback: raw text parsing with regex
Notes fallback may miss metadata
Notes Codex failure is non-fatal

evals.prompt: Explain the file-based handoff pattern between phases. What files are written and read between phases? How does this enable phase independence?
evals.expected_output: File-based handoff between phases: Phase 0 writes: changed_files.txt (file list), diff-ranges.json (line ranges). Phase 0.3 writes: context-intel.json. Phase 0.4 writes: linter-context.json. Phase 0.5 writes: risk-map.json, lore-analysis.md. Phase 2 writes: inscription.json (combines all above), state file .rune-review-{id}.json. Phase 3 reads: inscription.json, changed-files.txt. Phase 4 reads: signal directory, expected files. Phase 5 reads: all Ash output files. Phase 7 writes: TOME.md, echoes. Phase independence: each phase only reads files from previous phases, never directly calls functions. This enables: (1) Crash recovery — can resume from last written file, (2) Debugging — inspect intermediate state, (3) Parallel development — phases can be tested in isolation. The pattern follows the 'files as API' principle.
expectations: Lists Phase 0 outputs: changed_files.txt, diff-ranges.json
Lists Phase 0.3: context-intel.json
Lists Phase 0.4: linter-context.json
Lists Phase 0.5: risk-map.json, lore-analysis.md
Lists Phase 2: inscription.json, state file
Notes Phase 3 reads inscription.json
Explains independence: files as API, crash recovery
